1:
"Then went Boaz up to the gate, and sat him down there: and, behold, the kinsman of whom Boaz spake came by; unto whom he said, Ho, such a one! turn aside, sit down here. And he turned aside, and sat down." The very brief verse notes Boaz going to the gate and calling the nearer kinsman to sit. The grammatical voices note the gate. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Boaz went up to the gate, and sat him down there: and, behold, the kinsman of whom Boaz spake came by... Ho, such a one! turn aside, sit down here" — Boaz went to the gate, the place of judgment, and called the kinsman; the then went Boaz up to the gate, and sat him down there (the gate); the and, behold, the kinsman of whom Boaz spake came by; unto whom he said, Ho, such a one! turn aside, sit down here. And he turned aside, and sat down (the kinsman called); the figure (the redeemer going to the place of public justice to settle the matter openly; the truth that Boaz went to the gate, the place of legal business, to redeem openly, Deuteronomy 25:7; the figure of the redemption transacted in due and open order; the gate of justice; the kinsman called, vv.3:13). The verse holds together the gate.

6:
"And the kinsman said, I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance: redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it." The witnesses note the nearer kinsman declining to redeem, lest he mar his own inheritance, and yielding his right to Boaz — the crux of the law that cannot redeem, giving place to the redeemer who can and will. On "I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance: redeem thou my right to thyself," interpreters expound the refusal. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the failure of the first kinsman that gives place to the true redeemer — "and the kinsman said, I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance: redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it": the nearer kinsman, fearing loss to his own estate, declines and yields his right to Boaz; in this the witnesses see a figure: the first and nearer claimant (often read as the law, or the first covenant, or any redeemer who would gain without cost) cannot accomplish the redemption, and must yield to the true Redeemer who alone is able and willing to pay the full price (the law that could not redeem, weak through the flesh; the kinsman who would redeem only without cost or loss; the true Redeemer who counts no cost too great); the truth that the true Redeemer redeems not by sparing himself but by paying the full price, unlike the kinsman who drew back, "ye were not redeemed with corruptible things... but with the precious blood of Christ," 1 Peter 1:18-19; The figure of the law that cannot redeem, giving place to the Redeemer who can and will.

On "and the kinsman said, I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the kinsman drawing back for fear of loss. Keil and Ellicott note the nearer kinsman declining for fear of marring his own inheritance, unwilling to bear the cost of redeeming with Ruth (the redeemer who will not bear the cost, Romans 8:3) — the refusal (I cannot redeem it).

On "redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the right yielded to Boaz. The verse displays the kinsman declining and yielding his right to Boaz.

The verse holds together the custom. The verse displays the ancient and recognized form by which such transactions were confirmed and made binding, the drawing off of the shoe serving as a public testimony. The verse displays the truth that God's people transacted their affairs in due and recognized order, with sure confirmation.

10:
"Moreover Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, have I purchased to be my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead be not cut off from among his brethren, and from the gate of his place: ye are witnesses this day." The witnesses note Boaz declaring that he had purchased Ruth to be his wife, to raise up the dead's name — the crux of the redeemer who takes his bride at a price, joining her to himself and restoring her inheritance. On "Ruth the Moabitess... have I purchased to be my wife, to raise up the name of the dead," interpreters expound the redemption. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the redeemer who buys and weds his bride — "moreover Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, have I purchased to be my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance... ye are witnesses this day": Boaz declares he has bought Ruth to be his wife and to restore the dead's name and inheritance; the Gentile Ruth brought into the covenant family; the dead's name and inheritance restored through the marriage); the truth that Christ redeems his church as a bride, purchasing her to himself and joining her to him, "Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it... that he might present it to himself," Ephesians 5:25-27; The figure of the redeemer who takes his bride at a price, joining her to himself and restoring her inheritance.

On "Moreover Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, have I purchased to be my wife," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the bride purchased. Calvin and Keil note Boaz purchasing Ruth herself to be his wife, paying the full price for the person and not the land only, the Gentile brought into the covenant family, the redeemer who buys his bride (the bride bought with a price, 1 Corinthians 6:19-20) — the purchase (Ruth purchased to be my wife).

On "to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead be not cut off... ye are witnesses this day," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the dead's name restored. The verse displays Boaz purchasing Ruth to be his wife and restoring the dead's name.

The verse displays the redeemed-bride crux. The verse displays the people blessing the marriage and Ruth like Rachel and Leah (vv.11-12).

The verse displays the line carried through Boaz, the kinsman-redeemer, and Obed, the child of Ruth's redemption, the very names at the heart of this story now set in the line toward David. The verse displays the truth that the redemption wrought in Bethlehem was a link in the chain that God was forging toward the Messiah.

On "And Salmon begat Boaz," the witnesses note the descent. Gill notes Salmon begetting Boaz the redeemer (the line, Ruth 4:17) — the descent (Salmon to Boaz).

On "and Boaz begat Obed," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the continuation: "Boaz begat Obed" — the line to Obed, the son of Ruth (the descent); the figure (the line carried on through the redeemer and the redeemed); the providence (the descent, the line continued to Jesse and David to follow, vv.22). The verse displays the line from Salmon to Obed.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the descent — "Salmon begat Boaz, and Boaz begat Obed," the descent (Keil, Gill); "And Salmon begat Boaz," the descent (Ruth 4:17, Gill); "and Boaz begat Obed," the continuation (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the descent. The verse marks Salmon: begetting Boaz. The verse displays Boaz: the redeemer. The verse displays Obed: the son of Ruth. The verse displays the line: continued. The descent displays the line carried on through the redeemer and the redeemed. The verse displays the redemption: a link. The verse displays the generations: preserved. The verse displays the Redeemer: in view. The line from Salmon to Obed displays the line carried on through the redeemer and the redeemed: Salmon begat Boaz, and Boaz begat Obed (Ruth 4:17) — the line passing through Boaz the redeemer and Obed the son of Ruth, the line carried on through the redeemer and the redeemed. The verse displays the line completed to Jesse and David (vv.22).
22:
"And Obed begat Jesse, and Jesse begat David." The very brief verse notes the line completed from Obed to Jesse and David. The grammatical voices note the conclusion. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Obed begat Jesse, and Jesse begat David" — the genealogy ends at David, the goal of the whole book; the and Obed begat Jesse (Obed to Jesse); the and Jesse begat David (Jesse to David); the figure (the line brought to David, the king of God's choosing and the ancestor of Christ; the truth that the line reached David, the goal of the genealogy, 1 Samuel 16:13; the figure of the line of redemption brought to David and so to Christ; the line completed; David the goal, vv.17). The verse closes the book of Ruth. The verse displays the whole genealogy brought to its goal in David, the king after God's own heart and the head of the royal line from which the Messiah would come. The verse displays the truth that the sweet history of Ruth was, in God's providence, the story of the building of the line of David and of Christ.

On "And Obed begat Jesse," the witnesses note the descent. Gill notes Obed begetting Jesse (the line, 1 Samuel 16:13) — the descent (Obed to Jesse).

On "and Jesse begat David," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note David the goal: "Jesse begat David" — the line reaching David, the king and ancestor of Christ (the goal); the figure (the line of redemption brought to David and so to Christ); the providence (the conclusion, the book closed with David, the head of the royal line). The verse displays the line completed in David.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conclusion — "Obed begat Jesse, and Jesse begat David," the conclusion (Keil, Gill); "And Obed begat Jesse," the descent (1 Samuel 16:13, Gill); "and Jesse begat David," David the goal (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the conclusion. The verse marks Obed: begetting Jesse. The verse displays Jesse: begetting David. The verse displays David: the goal. The verse displays the line: completed. The conclusion displays the line of redemption brought to David and so to Christ. The verse displays the king: of God's choosing. The verse displays the royal line: established. The verse displays the Redeemer: in view. The line completed in David displays the line of redemption brought to David and so to Christ: Obed begat Jesse, and Jesse begat David (1 Samuel 16:13) — the genealogy ending at David, the king after God's own heart and the head of the royal line from which the Messiah would come, the line of redemption brought to David and so to Christ. The verse closes the book of Ruth, the story of the building of the line of David and of Christ.
